cross_connections.display_label: Susan Pierpont Edwards married Samuel William Johnson, son of Founding.

cross_connections.notes: William Samuel Johnson Sr. chaired the Committee of Style that drafted the final wording of the US Constitution.
cross_connections.display_label: Susan Pierpont Edwards married Samuel William Johnson, son of Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Aaron Burr's father-figure Pierpont Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Susan Pierpont Edwards was the daughter of Pierpont Edwards — Yale-trained Connecticut jurist, executor of the Aaron Burr estate following the death of Aaron Burr Sr., and grandson of Jonathan Edwards. Through Pierpont Edwards the line ran back via Mary Hooker Pierpont to Rev. Thomas Hooker. Susan was born in 1773 in Connecticut and died in February 1856 in Stratford at about age eighty-two or eighty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Constitution Committee of Style family
narrative_blocks.body: Susan Pierpont Edwards married Samuel William Johnson on November 27, 1791 in Stratford — the son of Founding Father William Samuel Johnson, chairman of the U.S. Constitution's Committee of Style. Their son William Samuel Johnson (1795-1883) later married Laura Woolsey, herself a Hooker descendant through a separate Edwards line, reuniting two branches of the family in the next generation. The Johnson-Edwards alliance braided the Hooker tree into the founding-era Connecticut elite.

notable.notable_blurb: First US District Judge CT; Grand Master CT

narrative_blocks.header: Jefferson named him Connecticut's first federal judge
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Jefferson appointed him the first U.S. District Judge for the District of Connecticut in 1806; he served until his death twenty years later. He had earlier represented Connecticut in the Continental Congress (1787-1788) and helped ratify the U.S. Constitution at the state convention.

His daughter Henrietta Frances Edwards married inventor Eli Whitney in 1817. Their daughter Henrietta Whitney Edwards later married Prof. Worthington Hooker in 1855 — reuniting the Hooker and Edwards lines through Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew Aaron Burr was six years younger
narrative_blocks.body: His older sister Esther Edwards married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr.; their son Aaron Burr Jr. became Vice President and was only six years younger than Pierpont. The two were uncle and nephew but more like brothers in age.

narrative_blocks.header: First Grand Master of Masons in Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: He was a Freemason in Hiram Lodge No. 1 in New Haven and became the first Grand Master of the Grand Lodge of Connecticut. The Grand Lodge still honors distinguished members with a medal in his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Richfield Township in Ohio's Western Reserve
narrative_blocks.body: He invested £60,000 in the Connecticut Land Company in 1795 — five percent of the company — and was one of seven investors who purchased Richfield Township in Ohio. He sent his son John Stark Edwards as his first agent in the Western Reserve.

cross_connections.display_label: Newton settled the Ohio township he had bought.
